Prince William remains the most popular royal, with 76% of Britons holding a positive opinion of him, followed closely by Princess Catherine at 73%. Meanwhile, the spare's popularity has dropped to 30% & Meghan "Respect my Authoritah!" Cartman favorability has also hit a record low at 21%. The trailer for part deux has 51K dislikes and counting. Newly released figures from Netflix show it has been watched 5.3 million times and ranks it number 383 in the list of the streaming service’s most-viewed shows. On Metacritic it has an average of 40 out of 100 (Generally Unfavorable). Additionally, the IMDb user rating currently still sits at 3.2/10 while the Rotten Tomatoes scores are 27% Avg. Tomatometer vs. 30% Avg. Popcornmeter for this series. Critical Reception: The Guardian critic Lucy Mangan slammed the series as “so painfully contrived that it’s genuinely fascinating.” Mangan called the series “boring,” but by the end, “you find yourself wondering things you would never normally wonder, such as, what would it take for me to embark on making vegan macaroons?” The Times of London critic Hilary Rose urged viewers to “start therapy now,” stating it “never becomes apparent why we should be interested in watching” any of Meghan’s culinary or fashion pursuits. Rose called the series “baffling,” stating it “occupies the sweet spot where irrelevant meets intolerable” and is “an advert for somewhere we’ll never go and aren’t invited.” The Telegraph’s Anita Singh criticized the series for “tone-deafness” and branded Markle a “Montecito Marie Antoinette,” stating she “emerges from her house to find that the production team have created an ‘impromptu flower mart’” for her, suggesting the show is inauthentic. The Independent roasted “With Love, Meghan” in a one-star review, stating watching the show is like being “gaslit by a multimillionaire,” noting “recipes are only half explained” as if “they already know that no one in their right mind is going to attempt this stuff.” Viewership: The second season failed to crack Netflix’s Top 10 chart during its debut week in late August 2025. And it was watched by fewer than 1.11 million viewers in its first two days. Furthermore their $100 million Netflix deal is coming to an end this September due to underwhelming viewership on recent projects. Netflix executives said the series "didn't maintain strong engagement." The spare's documentary Polo ranked far below expectations, landing at No. 3,436 on Netflix’s charts. Critical Reception: The Guardian critic Lucy Mangan slammed the series as “so painfully contrived that it’s genuinely fascinating.” Mangan called the series “boring,” but by the end, “you find yourself wondering things you would never normally wonder, such as, what would it take for me to embark on making vegan macaroons?” The Times of London critic Hilary Rose urged viewers to “start therapy now,” stating it “never becomes apparent why we should be interested in watching” any of Meghan’s culinary or fashion pursuits. Rose called the series “baffling,” stating it “occupies the sweet spot where irrelevant meets intolerable” and is “an advert for somewhere we’ll never go and aren’t invited.” The Telegraph’s Anita Singh criticized the series for “tone-deafness” and branded Markle a “Montecito Marie Antoinette,” stating she “emerges from her house to find that the production team have created an ‘impromptu flower mart’” for her, suggesting the show is inauthentic. The Independent roasted “With Love, Meghan” in a one-star review, stating watching the show is like being “gaslit by a multimillionaire,” noting “recipes are only half explained” as if “they already know that no one in their right mind is going to attempt this stuff.” Viewership: The second season failed to crack Netflix’s Top 10 chart during its debut week in late August 2025. And it was watched by fewer than 1.11 million viewers in its first two days. Furthermore their $100 million Netflix deal is coming to an end this September due to underwhelming viewership on recent projects. comment_text: To all the Meghansheeple, the Royal favorability for October 2025 is out! Prince William remains the most popular royal, with 76% of Britons holding a positive opinion of him, followed closely by Princess Catherine at 73%. Meanwhile, the spare's popularity has dropped to 30% & Meghan "Respect my Authoritah!" Cartman favorability has also hit a record low at 21%. The trailer for part deux has 50K dislikes and counting. Newly released figures from Netflix show it has been watched 5.3 million times and ranks it number 383 in the list of the streaming service’s most-viewed shows. On Metacritic it has an average of 40 out of 100 (Generally Unfavorable). Additionally, the IMDb user rating currently still sits at 3.2/10 while the Rotten Tomatoes scores are 27% Avg. Tomatometer vs. 30% Avg. Popcornmeter for this series. Critical Reception: The Guardian critic Lucy Mangan slammed the series as “so painfully contrived that it’s genuinely fascinating.” Mangan called the series “boring,” but by the end, “you find yourself wondering things you would never normally wonder, such as, what would it take for me to embark on making vegan macaroons?” The Times of London critic Hilary Rose urged viewers to “start therapy now,” stating it “never becomes apparent why we should be interested in watching” any of Meghan’s culinary or fashion pursuits. Rose called the series “baffling,” stating it “occupies the sweet spot where irrelevant meets intolerable” and is “an advert for somewhere we’ll never go and aren’t invited.” The Telegraph’s Anita Singh criticized the series for “tone-deafness” and branded Markle a “Montecito Marie Antoinette,” stating she “emerges from her house to find that the production team have created an ‘impromptu flower mart’” for her, suggesting the show is inauthentic. The Independent roasted “With Love, Meghan” in a one-star review, stating watching the show is like being “gaslit by a multimillionaire,” noting “recipes are only half explained” as if “they already know that no one in their right mind is going to attempt this stuff.” Viewership: The second season failed to crack Netflix’s Top 10 chart during its debut week in late August 2025. And it was watched by fewer than 1.11 million viewers in its first two days. Furthermore their $100 million Netflix deal is coming to an end this September due to underwhelming viewership on recent projects. Netflix executives said the series "didn't maintain strong engagement." The spare's documentary Polo ranked far below expectations, landing at No. 3,436 on Netflix’s charts. video_title: With Love, Meghan | Season 2 Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text: I’ve never watched this show or even a clip until this one. I’m touched and left in an unexpected  moment of nostalgia. It’s hard to put into words, butttt the visuals of this show remind me of how I would escape for hours flipping through the pages of my mom’s ‘Southern Living’ and ‘Better Homes and Gardens’ magazines followed by endlessly circling all my back to school clothes and toys we would never be able to buy in the ‘Sears Catalog Wish Book’. As a little black girl in the south the journals gave me a ticket of vibes and imagination to enter spaces I may not have been welcomed in real life. The feeling of exclusion while looking at those magazines never once not once entered my minds eye. I was too busy decorating my dream home and gardens. This show, in my opinion, seems like it could be like those magazines brought to life. Imagine if this show has the same effect for a little girl today, encouraging her to give herself permission to imagine her bespoke life keeping company with Barefoot Contessa, Martha, and Julia Child. The YouTuber Shann Bailey seems to also exist in that space. Enjoy her content too. Anyway, I don’t have Netflix, but I just might get it to watch this show. My momma would have loved this I think.

video_title: With Love, Meghan | Season 2 Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
Here’s a direct comparison between Pamela Anderson’s Cooking With Love and Meghan Markle’s Netflix show, showing why one soared while the other flopped:

1. Authenticity vs. Stagecraft

Pamela: Films in her real home, cooks in her own kitchen, invites actual family and friends. Every interaction feels organic.

Meghan: Guests are people she barely knows; scenes feel staged and overproduced. Authenticity is missing, leaving the show hollow.

2. Relatable Personality vs. Forced Relatability

Pamela: Funny, warm, and approachable without trying. Viewers feel a personal connection.

Meghan: Appears awkward and distant, often seeming to “perform” for the camera rather than engage naturally.

3. Accessible Content vs. Celebrity-Driven Concept

Pamela: Recipes are simple, doable, and educational. Her show feels inclusive to home cooks.

Meghan: Focused on celebrity appeal and glamour rather than practical cooking; audiences can’t connect with it.

4. Emotional Connection vs. Hollow Presentation

Pamela: Family and friend interactions create warmth, laughter, and intimacy.

Meghan: Lacks genuine emotional moments; viewers notice the absence of real bonds, making it feel artificial.
